content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95814 • 1101 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance • [illegible] • Enforce Legis • [illegible] • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660       322-3751        322-5441        322-6844

March 27, 1978

Mr. H. Richard Marsh
Knapp, Stevens, Grosman and Marsh
1800 United California Bank Bldg.
707 Wilshire Boulevard
Los Angeles, California 90017

Dear Mr. Marsh:

This is in response to your letter of January 25, 1978, requesting advice concerning the filing requirements for members of groups wishing to nominate members of the Los Angeles County Board of Supervisors in their capacity as members of alternate members of the Board of Directors of certain county sanitation districts. The Commission will not issue a formal opinion in this matter; however, you may from the following notice issued pursuant to Government Code §11410.

Initially, I would direct your attention to Section 2 of the Conflict of Interest Code of the Board of Supervisors of Los Angeles County. It provides that:

"This Conflict of Interest Code shall be applicable to members of the Board of Supervisors, as members of the Board of Supervisors and as ex officio members of districts, agencies, authorities and commissions."

To the extent that the supervisors in question fit within this provision, they are covered by the Board's Code and need not be covered under the codes of the sanitation districts in question. Furthermore, Government Code §97019 provides in relevant part that, "The provision of a Conflict of Interest Code adopted under this section shall not be applicable to any designated employee who is covered by Article 2 of this chapter." This provision dictates a conclusion that disclosure by members of the county sanitation districts who are

A-78-255

[illegible]

re. Richard Marsh
March 27, 1978                                                    Page Two


also supervisors is not appropriate in the context of the
Code for the sanitation district.* As you suggest in your
letter, I would recommend an appropriate amendment.

I believe that this analysis would also answer your
second question. However, there are additional reasons
for finding that the supervisor need not file such State-
ments. You indicate in your letter that the supervisor
is sitting on the [illegible] by virtue of his role as a
supervisor. He has not left nor assumed that office.
Accordingly, the requirements of Government Code §87204
have not attached.

I trust this answers your questions. Please forgive
my delay in responding. If I can be of further assistance
in this or any other matter, please feel free to call on me
at (916) 332-6344.


                                                  Sincerely,




                                                  Kenneth N. Goshorn
                                                  Deputy Specialist III
                                                  Conflicts of Interest
                                                  Division

KNG:db


*The Commission has limited this interpretation to Codes
for districts within the same "jurisdiction". See Government
Code §82013. Such is the case in this situation.

January 25, 1978


Mr. Kenneth Goshorn, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

RE: Political Reform Act of 1974--Disclosure Statements
     County Sanitation Districts

Dear Mr. Goshorn:

     This will supplement our recent telephone dis-
cussions concerning disclosure requirements of members
of the Los Angeles County Board of Supervisors when they
act in the capacity of members or alternate members of
the Board of Directors of a County Sanitation District
of Los Angeles County.

     Within this County there are 27 County Sanitation Districts, all of which have been formed pursuant
to the provisions of the County Sanitation District
Act (Chapter 3 Part 1 Division 5 of the Health and Safety
Code, §4700 et seq.). B.S 4700 provides that the presiding officer of the county board of supervisors is a
member of the board of directors of any district that
includes unincorporated territory. That section also
provides that if the district includes no territory which
is in cities or sanitary districts, then the county board
of supervisors is the board of directors of the district.
In Los Angeles County the latter provision applies to
five Sanitation Districts and the former to 18 Sanitation
Districts.

     Each of the County Sanitation Districts of Los
Angeles County has adopted an identical Conflict of Interest

Mr. Kenneth Goshorn, Esq.
January 25, 1978
re: Political Reform Act of 1974 - Disclosure Statements
      County Sanitation Districts

Code in a form suggested by the County with respect to
special districts concerning which it acts as the code
reviewing body. A copy of this Uniform Code is enclosed.

In light of the situation described above I
would appreciate your opinion on the following questions:

1. Assuming that all Sanitation Districts are
located within the County of Los Angeles and having in
mind the disclosure requirements provided for by Article 2
of Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act of 1974, is there any
reason why the Supervisors' disclosure requirements as
Directors or alternate Directors of certain Sanitation
Districts could not be satisfied by the filing of a dupli-
cate of the disclosure statement required of them in their
capacity as Supervisors? Since the original of both
documents will be filed with the same County Officer, is
there any reason why even this requirement could not be
eliminated? In your revised draft, it would
presume that an amendment to the enclosed Code would be
necessary?

2. The following are more specific questions
and involve the following factual situations:

The immediate past Chairman of the Board
of Supervisors served as a Director of 13 Sanitation
Districts throughout 1977, until December 6, 1977, at which
time the Board of Supervisors was reorganized. (There is
in addition to the five Sanitation Districts to which
the Board of Supervisors acts as a Board of Directors).
At that time a different Chairman was elected who became,
by operation of law, a Director of those same Districts.
On December 20, 1977, this former Chairman of the Board of
Supervisors was designated by that Board as an alternate
Director for those same Districts. The question posed is
whether or not the provisions of the Act should be so
strictly construed as to require the filing of a closing
statement within 30 days of December 6, 1977, and an
initial statement on December 20, 1977. As indicated
to you on preliminary draft, even if there the appointee
for an alternate were with in 30 days of his initial

Mr. Kenneth Coshorn, Esq.
January 25, 1978
re: Political Reform Act of 1974 - Disclosure Statements
     County Sanitation Districts

the office of Chairman that a termination and new initial
statement should not be required so long as the annual
statement is filed.  The annual statement here should be
prepared on the assumption that there has been continuity
of office throughout the year.

b.  For some period of time the Board of Directors of District No. 14 had been composed of the Chairman
and one other member of the Board of Supervisors.  Upon
the recent incorporation of Lancaster the Mayor of the
City became a Director and the second member of the Board
of Supervisors was eliminated as a member of the Board.

In view of the broader disclosure requirements
imposed on supervisors is there a need for a new member
to file a closing statement?

You have advised me by telephone that in any
event no closing statements or new initial statements
would be needed until we have received your opinion. If
you need any further information from us on these matters,
don't hesitate to advise me.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        B. RICHARD MARSH
                                        District Counsel

cc: Mr. Schabarum
    Mr. Edelman
    Mr. Hahn
    Mr. Ward
    Mr. Hayes
    CCO Mr. Pozorski

Enclosure

sections.question: 1. Assuming that all Sanitation Districts are located within the County of Los Angeles and having in mind the disclosure requirements provided for by Article 2 of Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act of 1974, is there any reason why the Supervisors' disclosure requirements as Directors or alternates Directors of certain Sanitation Districts could not be satisfied by the filing of a duplicate of the disclosure statement required of them in their capacity as Supervisors? Since the original of both documents will be filed with the same County Officer, is there any reason why even this requirement could not be eliminated? 2. [Specific questions regarding: (a) Whether a closing statement and new initial statement are required when an officer transition and alternate appointment occur within 30 days; and (b) Whether a closing statement is required when a supervisor is removed from a sanitation district board due to city incorporation.]
sections.conclusion: To the extent that supervisors fit within Section 2 of the Conflict of Interest Code of the Board of Supervisors (which provides the Code applies to members as ex officio members of districts, agencies, and commissions), they are covered by the Board's Code and need not be covered under the Codes of the sanitation districts. Government Code §87302 dictates that disclosure by sanitation district members who are supervisors is not appropriate in the context of the sanitation district Code. Additionally, Government Code §87204 does not attach filing requirements where a supervisor has not left nor assumed office—i.e., where there is continuity of role.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Los Angeles County Board of Supervisors members must file separate Statements of Economic Interest when serving on County Sanitation District boards, or whether their existing supervisor disclosures satisfy the requirement; and whether specific filing requirements apply in cases of officer transitions and removals.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Los Angeles County Supervisors serving on County Sanitation District boards are covered by the Board of Supervisors' Conflict of Interest Code as ex officio members and need not file separate disclosure statements under the sanitation districts' codes. No closing or initial statements are required where there is continuity of the supervisory role, even if the specific sanitation district assignments change.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request (January 25, 1978 letter from B. Richard Marsh) and the FPPC response (March 27, 1978 letter from Kenneth W. Goshorn). Response is formal FPPC advice issued pursuant to Government Code §83114(b). Contains reference note regarding interpretation limited to districts within same jurisdiction under §82035. Minor date discrepancy in incoming request header (1973 vs 1978) appears to be typographical error in original document.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Assuming that all Sanitation Districts are located within the County of Los Angeles and having in mind the disclosure requirements provided for by Article 2 of Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act of 1974, is there any reason why the Supervisors' disclosure requirements as Directors or alternates Directors of certain Sanitation Districts could not be satisfied by the filing of a duplicate of the disclosure statement required of them in their capacity as Supervisors? Since the original of both documents will be filed with the same County Officer, is there any reason why even this requirement could not be eliminated? 2. [Specific questions regarding: (a) Whether a closing statement and new initial statement are required when an officer transition and alternate appointment occur within 30 days; and (b) Whether a closing statement is required when a supervisor is removed from a sanitation district board due to city incorporation.]

CONCLUSION: To the extent that supervisors fit within Section 2 of the Conflict of Interest Code of the Board of Supervisors (which provides the Code applies to members as ex officio members of districts, agencies, and commissions), they are covered by the Board's Code and need not be covered under the Codes of the sanitation districts. Government Code §87302 dictates that disclosure by sanitation district members who are supervisors is not appropriate in the context of the sanitation district Code. Additionally, Government Code §87204 does not attach filing requirements where a supervisor has not left nor assumed office—i.e., where there is continuity of role.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that Los Angeles County Supervisors serving on County Sanitation District boards satisfy their disclosure requirements through their supervisor disclosures under the Board's Conflict of Interest Code, and need not file separate statements with the sanitation districts, with no special filing requirements for role transitions involving continuity.
